title: Time-Series Database (TimescaleDB)
description: TimescaleDB is a PostgreSQL extension for time-series data. Combines relational and time-series capabilities with automatic partitioning (hypertables). Best for: combining time-series with relational data, using SQL for time-series queries, self-hosted deployments. Leverages PostgreSQL ecosystem (extensions, tools, backups).

provides.reasoning: TimescaleDB's primary purpose is storing and managing time-series data through hypertables with automatic partitioning. This is its core functionality.

provides.description: Use standard SQL for time-series analysis
provides.reasoning: As a PostgreSQL extension, TimescaleDB provides full SQL query capabilities for time-series data, which is a key differentiator from other time-series databases.
Continuous aggregates are a core TimescaleDB feature for materialized views that automatically refresh, essential for time-series analytics and downsampling.
TimescaleDB is built on PostgreSQL and provides full relational database capabilities alongside time-series features, enabling the combination of time-series with relational data.
TimescaleDB offers native compression for time-series data, which is a significant feature for managing storage costs and performance in production deployments.
Hypertables are TimescaleDB's specific automatic partitioning mechanism that abstracts time-series data management and is fundamental to its architecture.
TimescaleDB offers automated data retention management which is important for time-series use cases to manage storage and comply with data lifecycle requirements.
TimescaleDB is a PostgreSQL extension; it inherits full native RLS support via CREATE POLICY, enabling per-tenant time-series data isolation.
TimescaleDB is built on PostgreSQL and inherits full PREPARE TRANSACTION support for XA distributed transaction coordination.

requires.reasoning: TimescaleDB is a PostgreSQL extension and cannot function without PostgreSQL as its foundation. It requires PostgreSQL to be installed and running.

retention_days.description: Data retention policy in days. Older chunks are automatically deleted. Trade-off: longer retention = more storage costs vs. historical data availability

chunk_time_interval_hours.description: Hypertable chunk interval: 1=hourly (very high throughput), 24=daily (high throughput), 168=weekly (standard), 720=monthly (low throughput). Smaller chunks = better for recent data queries, larger = better compression

compression_enabled.description: Enable TimescaleDB native compression (10x-20x storage reduction). Trade-off: compressed chunks are read-only but dramatically reduce costs

continuous_aggregates_enabled.description: Enable continuous aggregates for automatic downsampling (hourly/daily rollups). Trade-off: faster queries on historical data vs. additional write overhead

replication_factor.description: Number of data replicas: 1=no replication (single node), 2=high availability, 3=maximum durability. Trade-off: higher availability vs. storage/compute costs

max_connections.description: Maximum concurrent database connections. Trade-off: higher limit supports more clients but increases memory usage (each connection ~10MB)

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Min $0 represents free self-hosted deployment on existing infrastructure or TimescaleDB Cloud free tier (up to 30 days data retention, limited storage). Low-range production starts at $50-100 for small self-hosted instances (basic VPS or cloud VM with 2-4 vCPUs, 8-16GB RAM). Mid-range $300-500 covers managed TimescaleDB Cloud services for moderate workloads (multi-GB daily ingestion, standard compression). Max $2000 represents larger production deployments with high ingestion rates (millions of data points/day), significant storage (1-5TB), read replicas for analytics, and automated backups. Enterprise-scale deployments with extensive data retention can exceed this range significantly.
provenance.adoptionCost: Medium-high complexity adoption. Requires understanding of: (1) PostgreSQL administration and tuning, (2) TimescaleDB-specific concepts (hypertables, continuous aggregates, compression policies, retention policies), (3) Time-series data modeling best practices (indexing strategies, chunk sizing), (4) Migration from existing time-series solutions if applicable. Team needs 1-2 weeks for: learning TimescaleDB extensions, designing schema with proper time-series partitioning, implementing compression and retention policies, setting up monitoring and backup strategies, and load testing. Lower than pure distributed time-series databases due to PostgreSQL familiarity, but higher than basic PostgreSQL due to specialized time-series optimization knowledge required.
provenance.licenseCost: TimescaleDB Community Edition is fully open source (Apache 2.0 license) with core features including hypertables, continuous aggregates, compression, and data retention policies available at no cost. Can be self-hosted indefinitely without licensing fees. Managed TimescaleDB Cloud is usage-based (pay-as-you-go) rather than requiring upfront licenses. Enterprise features in Timescale license are optional add-ons, not required for standard time-series workloads. No per-core, per-server, or seat-based licensing.

supports_nfr.reason: Self-hosted TimescaleDB with proper replication and failover can achieve up to four nines availability.
TimescaleDB as a PostgreSQL-based relational database achieves p95 latency floor of 5ms for typical queries.
TimescaleDB p99 latency floor is 20ms due to PostgreSQL query processing and hypertable chunking overhead.
With streaming replication and automatic failover, TimescaleDB achieves RTO of at least 1 minute.
TimescaleDB can handle up to 100k inserts/second sustained (360M writes per hour) with optimized hypertables.
TimescaleDB supports up to 50k read queries per second with proper indexing and continuous aggregates.
TimescaleDB optimized for time-series writes can handle up to 100k write queries per second with batching.
TimescaleDB inherits PostgreSQL's ACID guarantees with WAL, supporting both strict and relaxed durability modes.
TimescaleDB provides strong consistency with read-your-writes guarantees as a PostgreSQL-based system.
TimescaleDB supports comprehensive audit logging through PostgreSQL's pgaudit extension when required.
TimescaleDB supports GDPR compliance through PostgreSQL features including encryption, retention policies, and data deletion.
TimescaleDB can meet HIPAA requirements with proper configuration including encryption, audit logging, and access controls.
TimescaleDB supports CCPA compliance through data retention, deletion capabilities, and audit trails.
TimescaleDB supports SOX compliance through audit logging, access controls, data integrity, and backup capabilities.
TimescaleDB can handle PII data with encryption at rest and in transit, column-level security, and access controls.
TimescaleDB supports multiple tenant isolation strategies including row-level security, schema separation, and database-per-tenant.

supports_constraints.reason: TimescaleDB can be self-hosted on any cloud provider or on-premises infrastructure as a PostgreSQL extension.
TimescaleDB is database infrastructure accessible from any language with PostgreSQL client support.
TimescaleDB is backend infrastructure for time-series data storage supporting APIs, workers, batch jobs, data pipelines, and IoT workloads.
TimescaleDB pattern is specifically designed for time-series database workloads with hypertables and time-series optimizations.
TimescaleDB supports OLTP workloads as it's built on PostgreSQL and can combine transactional with time-series data.
TimescaleDB supports analytical queries on time-series data with continuous aggregates and compression for OLAP use cases.
TimescaleDB is a database pattern that can work with or without additional caching layers for query optimization.
TimescaleDB is compatible with applications that use async messaging for data ingestion or event-driven architectures.
TimescaleDB can store time-series data for AI inference applications and integrate with ML pipelines for feature engineering.
TimescaleDB supports multi-tenant architectures through row-level security, schema isolation, and database-per-tenant patterns.
TimescaleDB supports batch processing workloads for time-series data aggregation, compression, and continuous aggregates.
TimescaleDB supports distributed transactions through PostgreSQL's two-phase commit protocol when coordination is required.
TimescaleDB can ingest real-time streaming data and serve as storage backend for streaming analytics with low-latency writes.
TimescaleDB can leverage PostgreSQL vector extensions (pgvector) for combined time-series and vector similarity search.
TimescaleDB supports JSONB columns for document storage alongside time-series data in hybrid data models.
TimescaleDB can function as a key-value store using PostgreSQL's indexing capabilities for fast lookups with time-series context.
TimescaleDB can leverage PostgreSQL graph extensions (Apache AGE) for combined time-series and graph relationship queries.

warn_nfr.reason: TimescaleDB's hypertable partitioning and time-series optimizations add overhead that is not justified at very low write rates.
warn_nfr.message: db-timeseries--timescaledb: peak write QPS is {actual} req/s (<10). TimescaleDB's hypertable overhead may not be justified; consider simpler PostgreSQL tables or SQLite.

warn_nfr.reason: TimescaleDB is optimized for high-volume time-series ingestion. At very low ingestion rates, the complexity of hypertables and continuous aggregates is unnecessary.
warn_nfr.message: db-timeseries--timescaledb: peak ingestion is {actual} jobs/hour (<1000). TimescaleDB's time-series optimizations may be overkill; consider standard PostgreSQL or simpler storage.
